perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to disclose Confidential Information only to their respective employees, agents, or representatives who have been determined to have a need to know and have been advised of their obligation to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to disclose Confidential Information only to their respective employees, agents, or representatives, regardless of whether they have been determined to have a need to know, as long as they are advised of their obligation to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a 'need to know' determination before disclosing confidential information. Removing this requirement contradicts the general obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to broader disclosures than intended. This new clause directly conflicts with the intent of section 3 which speaks about the precautions that are necessary when handing confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party shall take such steps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent disclosure of the Confidential Information to third parties, but shall apply at least the same level of security as is afforded to the Receiving Party’s own confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'need to know' requirement before disclosing confidential information. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which mandates taking 'reasonably necessary steps to prevent disclosure,' implying a more restrictive approach to sharing confidential information. By allowing disclosure regardless of 'need to know,' the changed text weakens the precautions and creates a strong inconsistency regarding the appropriate handling of confidential information as defined by both sections. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where different sections provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will return or destroy Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party upon termination of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will return Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party upon termination of the Agreement, unless destruction is mutually agreed upon in writing by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement mandates the return *or* destruction of confidential information. The modified version makes the returning mandatory. This creates a direct contradiction as the original agreement allows for destruction as a valid option, while the modified section restricts that, thus, contradicting the text as is.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party will return or destroy Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party upon termination of the Agreement. The Receiving Party’s designated representative may maintain one copy of all Confidential Information for the purpose of addressing any claim that may be brought under this Agreement and to comply wit h any other legal or recordkeeping requirements, and neither party will be obligated to destroy any Confidential Information that is stored electronically on back -up systems or computer hard drives after a file is deleted, but any such electronic informati on will continue to be subject to the terms of confidentiality under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for either returning or destroying confidential information. The changed text mandates the return of confidential information unless destruction is mutually agreed upon, effectively removing the option of unilateral destruction initially present. The contradicted text, which mirrors the original text, allows for either return or destruction. Therefore, the changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text, making the original allowance of unilateral destruction incompatible. The modification narrows the options available to the Receiving Party, creating a clear inconsistency and a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the prior permission of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not be provided in any form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, regardless of legal requirements.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'unless otherwise required by law' clause creates a contradiction. The original allows disclosure if legally mandated. The modified version prohibits it even if legally required, creating an in-text contradiction. This change will effect section 9 as that mentions laws of Wisconsin and this change doesn't allow for laws and regulations to be followed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Wisconsin, U.S.A. without regard to conflicts of law provisions. In the event any legal action beco mes necessary to enforce, the Parties agree that such action will be brought in the U.S. District Court of Western District of Wisconsin, and the Parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of said courts
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Receiving Party to disclose Confidential Information if required by law. The changed text removes this exception, prohibiting disclosure 'regardless of legal requirements'. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which states the agreement is governed by the laws of Wisconsin. The changed text would prevent adherence to Wisconsin law if that law required disclosure, creating a direct conflict and therefore a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party expressly acknowledges that the Disclosing Party owns the Confidential Information they disclose, and that the transmission by the Disclosing Party of their Confidential Information (or any third party’s Confidential Information entrusted to the Disclosing Party) shall not be construed to grant the Receiving Party any patent, know -how, copyright, trade secret, trademark, or other intellectual property rights in, or arising from, the Confidential Information disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party expressly acknowledges that the Disclosing Party owns the Confidential Information they disclose; however, the Receiving Party may be granted limited intellectual property rights related to specific applications of the Confidential Information, subject to a separate written agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement explicitly states that no intellectual property rights are granted by disclosing confidential information. The modified version introduces the possibility of granting limited IP rights, contradicting the explicit denial of such rights in the original clause. This new clause is a direct contradiction to the first part of section 7 which says intellectual property is not granted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party expressly acknowledges that the Disclosing Party owns the Confidential Information they disclose, and that the transmission by the Disclosing Party of their Confidential Information (or any third party’s Confidential Information entrusted to the Disclosing Party) shall not be construed to grant the Receiving Party any patent, know -how, copyright, trade secret, trademark, or other intellectual property rights in, or arising from, the Confidential Information disclosed. If any such rights are to be granted to the Receiving Party, such grant shall be expressly set forth in a separate written instrument.
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text state that no intellectual property rights are granted through the disclosure of confidential information, unless explicitly stated in a separate written instrument. However, the changed text introduces the possibility of granting limited IP rights related to specific applications of the Confidential Information, subject to a separate written agreement. This directly contradicts the initial assertion that no such rights are granted and creates an in-text contradiction, as the agreement now contains conflicting statements about the granting of intellectual property rights, even if contingent on a further agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall begin on the date of the last signature on this Agreement and expire after one (1) year, unless terminated earlier by a party with thirty (30) days prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall begin on the date of the last signature on this Agreement and expire after one (1) year, but can only be terminated earlier by mutual written consent of both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement allows either party to terminate with 30 days' notice. The modified version requires mutual consent, contradicting the original unilateral termination option. This adds a direct in-text contradiction due to the requirement of written consent between both parties to terminate early, while the original excerpt allows termination to happen with a written notice in advance. This is contradicting the clause that provides the termination can happen with just a notice and no written consent needed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The term of this Agreement shall begin on the date of the last signature on this Agreement and expire after one (1) year, unless terminated earlier by a party with thirty (30) days prior written notice. The obligations and restrictions of confidentiality of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall continue for a period of three (3) years from the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text are the same, stating that the agreement can be terminated by either party with thirty days' notice. The changed text modifies this, requiring mutual written consent for termination, which directly contradicts the original unilateral termination option, creating a strong in-text contradiction as either party is no longer allowed to terminate the agreement with just a 30 day notice.
